fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

KARI J:

INTRODUCTION

At a hearing on 1 April 2026 the Court made a harmful proceedings order pursuant to s 102QAC of the Family Law Act (“the Act”) directed to the father in parenting proceedings.
At the time of making the harmful proceedings order the Court indicated to the parties and the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) that reasons for the making of that order would publish following the hearing.
These are those reasons.

BACKGROUND

The substantive proceedings between the parties concern the parenting arrangements for their now five year old child X born in 2021.
The proceedings were commenced by the father in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), on 15 December 2022 when the child was over 12 months old, at which time the father asked the Court to make a recovery order.
The parties to the proceedings are the child’s father who is 50 years of age, and the child’s mother who is 43 years of age.
At the first hearing in the proceedings on 21 December 2022 an order was made appointing an Independent Children’s Lawyer.
The parenting orders that have been made in the proceedings have ostensibly provided for a largely shared care arrangement, with orders originally made for the child to live between the parties with care rotating on a daily basis (orders made 21 December 2022). The rotation then moved to every second day (orders made 6 February 2026). On 22 March 2023 orders were made for the child to live with the mother and spend time with the father for defined periods each Monday, Wednesday and Saturday. Whilst the father’s day time spending on each of those days was varied by subsequent orders, those arrangements prevailed until orders were made at the invitation of the parties on 11 November 2025.
The parenting orders that are presently in place for the child are those made by consent at the hearing on 11 November 2025 following agreement reached between the parties at a Family Dispute Resolution conference at the Legal Services Commission of South Australia. Those orders provide for:
(a) The child to continue to live with the mother.

(b) The child to spend time with the father over a fortnightly rotation at specified times prior to the child commencing school in July 2026, with a variation to occur after the child commenced school.

(c) The arrangements that currently apply provide for the father to spend time with the child in week one from 9.00am to 5.00pm on Monday and from 9.00am Saturday until 5.00pm Sunday, and in week two from 9.00am Wednesday until the commencement of pre-school on Thursday and from 9.00am to 5.00pm on Saturday.

While these orders remain in place, the Court understands from documents recently filed in the proceedings and submissions made during the hearing on 1 April 2026, that from approximately Saturday 28 March 2026, the mother has not facilitated time spending between the father and the child, and that she intends to bring an application to suspend the father’s time spending as provided by the orders of 11 November 2025.
It is the mother’s case that her relationship with the father has been marred by family violence including coercive controlling behaviour towards her by the father. These allegations are denied by the father. Instead, he has made allegations that the mother has subjected him to “‘a massive amount’ of emotional/psychological abuse”, asserting that the mother was “demanding, controlling and excessively emotionally needy” and that the mother “engaged in non-consensual sexual practices which he found traumatising” (Child Impact Report dated 27 April 2023, paragraph 15).
In addition, it is apparent from the court file that concerns have existed regarding the mental health of each of the parents, with orders made 28 April 2023 for the father to provide a report from his treating psychiatrist (who he has asserted he has seen on a fortnightly basis for at least a decade) (Child Impact Report dated 27 April 2023, paragraph 28), and the mother to undertake an independent psychiatric assessment. Those reports were filed in the proceedings on 26 July 2023 by the ICL on behalf of the mother and 23 August 2023 by the father.
It does not appear to be in dispute that:
(a) The father was involved in a physical altercation with his mother in 2022 which took place in the presence of the child and resulted in police attendance and child protection involvement (DCP response dated December 2022, and Family Report dated 5 September 2024, paragraph 34).

(b) During interviews for the Child Impact Report and the Family Report prepared in these proceedings, the father asserted that he had “stayed overnight” at a “hospital emergency department” in late 2022 due to “‘fleeting’ thoughts of suicide in response to the current Court proceedings” (Child Impact Report dated 27 April 2023, paragraph 28). The father also discussed this hospital admission during his Family Report interview (at paragraph 56), with the Court Child Expert having had regard to notes produced from the hospital which identified the father had “significant suicidal thoughts” and that he had “plans to use carbon monoxide, a method of suicide which he had previously attempted in 2014” (Family Report dated 5 September 2024, paragraph 56).

(c) The father has reported being previously diagnosed with an “Adjustment Disorder”, “Depressed/Anxious Mood” and that he has been prescribed medication by his treating psychiatrist (Child Impact Report dated 27 April 2023, paragraph 28 and Family Report dated 5 September 2024, paragraph 55), together with being diagnosed with having “obsessional and paranoid traits” (Family Report dated 5 September 2024, paragraph 55).

On 1 February 2023 an order was made by a judicial registrar pursuant to s 91B of the Act inviting the Department for Child Protection to intervene in the proceedings. That order led to the department preparing a number of reports in the proceedings, with the initial view taken that the department would monitor and observe the child’s circumstances, before ultimately declining the Court’s request to intervene in the proceedings in May 2023, on the basis that the child was safe in the mother’s primary care, with concerns held regarding the father’s mental health.
On 27 April 2023 a Child Impact Report was prepared. At that time the Court Child Expert held significant concerns and considered the parties’ “mental health” to be a significant risk factor for the child (paragraph 33). The recommendations of the Court Child Expert at that time were (paragraphs 42-51):
(a) The then parenting arrangements remain in place, which at that time provided for the father to spend eight hours on three days of the week with the child (paragraph 3).

(b) The father to continue engaging with his psychiatrist Dr G “for oversight of his mental health concerns and support in establishing a viable co-parenting relationship with [the mother]”.

(c) The mother to engage with a psychologist “for support in addressing her concerns, unresolved issues in respect of her relationship with [the father], and to establish a viable co-parenting relationship with [the father].

(d) The Court to access additional collateral information from the parties’ treating practitioners in respect of their mental health.

(e) If the mother holds concerns that the father is under the influence of alcohol when the child is in the father’s care, the mother should be able to request him to complete a blood alcohol test.

On 5 September 2024 a Family Report was prepared by a Court Child Expert. The recommendations at that time were (paragraphs 93-97):
(a) The child ought reside with the mother;

(b) Sole decision making be given to the child’s primary carer (presumably the mother) “given the allegations and history of interparental conflict reducing timely decisions being made for [X] thus far”;

(c) The arrangements then in place for the child to spend time with the father ought continue (at that stage they were eight and a half hours on three occasions each week (paragraph 4)), with increases to be considered in the future, but depending on the child’s physical and emotional maturity and also the father’s mental health and the state of the co-parenting relationship;

(d) Given ongoing mental health concerns for each parent the parties were strongly recommended to “remain actively engaged with their respective practitioners”, to attempt to “decrease the chances of mental health issues interfering with parenting capacity”; and

(e) The parties needed to focus on improving their co-parenting relationship with the assistance of professional supports.

On 11 November 2025 the Court ordered the preparation of an updated Family Report. Appointments for that report are imminent.
From the Family Report dated 5 September 2024 it is apparent that:
(a) Family violence was identified as a risk factor (paragraph 39), noting that whilst the father made more vague allegations against the mother (paragraph 32), the mother made more detailed allegations about the cyclical nature of the verbal abuse she experienced from the father (paragraph 35).

(b) Significant concerns were held about the poor co-parenting relationship between the parties (paragraph 20).

(c) Concerns were held over the “impact of the father’s mental health on his parenting and its implications for [the child’s] well-being” (paragraph 20).

(d) Child safety concerns were identified as a risk factor (paragraph 44).

(e) The father’s alcohol consumption was identified as a possible risk factor (paragraph 50).

(f) The father’s mental health was identified as a significant risk factor (paragraph 59), with the mother describing the father’s mental state as a “rollercoaster” (paragraph 53), and describing the father’s behaviours to include fixation, interrogation of her, stalking tendencies, paranoia and exaggeration of minor issues (paragraph 53). The father on the other hand was “adamant” in his interview with the Court Child Expert that his mental health did not impair his parenting and that it enhanced his dedication and care for the child (paragraph 55).

PRELIMINARY OBSERVATION

A preliminary observation of the volume of material on the court file reveals that the proceedings have been highly contentious. There have been no less than 217 documents filed in the proceedings, and 59 separate orders made by the Court. Aside from the interlocutory orders sought by the parties when the proceedings were commenced, the father has filed eleven separate interlocutory applications in the proceedings; the mother has not filed any.
As part of these proceedings there have been three Review Applications filed by the father on each 27 February 2024, 28 June 2024 and 2 February 2026. Whilst the Application for Review filed in February 2024 was partially upheld, the balance of those review applications were dismissed.
In addition, it is apparent that the father has commenced/attempted to commence an appeal on three separate occasions in relation to orders made in these proceedings. To that end:
(a) A Notice of Appeal was filed on 23 March 2023, however the same was deemed abandoned pursuant to the provisions of r 13.14(3) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”), because the father failed to file a draft index in the appeal.

(b) On 24 March 2026 a Notice of Appeal was filed. That notice was not accepted for filing because it incompetently sought to appeal orders made in these proceedings on each 11 November 2025, 12 March 2026 and 16 March 2026.

(c) On 31 March 2026 a Notice of Appeal was filed. That notice was not accepted for filing in circumstances where the father had failed to lodge a competent fee exemption form or failed to pay the relevant filing fee.

This volume of litigation provides a graphic illustration as to why it is that the litigation remains before the Court over three years after the proceedings were commenced. The volume of the material filed in the litigation, and particularly that instigated by the father informs the issues to be discussed later in these reasons regarding the impact of these proceedings upon the mother.

THE CURRENT APPLICATION

The mother has asked the Court to make a harmful proceedings order directed to the father.
She did so on 27 February 2026 in a Response to an Application in a Proceeding, filed in reply to an application filed by the father on 22 December 2025.
Rather than repeating it here, the context in which that Response to an Application in a Proceeding was filed by the mother is more readily understood by having regard to the reasons published by me when orders were made on 30 March 2026 in these proceedings (Tordai & Aarle [2026] FedCFamC1F 203).
Those reasons concerned the dismissal by the Court of an Application in a Proceeding filed by the father on 20 March 2026, in which the father amongst other things, sought to adjourn the contested interlocutory hearing in these proceedings on 1 April 2026, together with an order that I be disqualified from hearing these proceedings on the basis of an apprehension of bias.
The dismissal of the father’s Application in a Proceeding filed 20 March 2026 meant that there were a number of interlocutory applications pending before the Court at the hearing on 1 April 2026 as follows:
(a) The father’s Application in a Proceeding filed 22 December 2025;

(b) The mother’s Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 27 February 2026;

(c) The father’s Application in a Proceeding filed 10 February 2026; and

(d) The father’s Application in a Proceeding filed 13 March 2026.

In addition, the day prior to the hearing on 31 March 2026:
(a) At 1.30pm the ICL filed a Response to an Application in a Proceeding; and

(b) At 4.30pm the father filed a further Application in a Proceeding.

Other than as to one order sought by the father in his application, both interlocutory applications filed on 31 March 2026 (by orders made on 1 April 2026) were listed for hearing on 29 April 2026, with orders made for the filing of responding material by each of the parents and the ICL. As the Court explained to all parties during the hearing, procedural fairness demanded that the respondents to each of those applications had the opportunity to consider, take advice and respond to the same prior to the Court’s determination.
The only order in each of those applications considered by the Court at the hearing on 1 April 2026 was that proposed by the father in his application of 31 March 2026 at paragraph 2, by which he sought:
That all further proceedings in matter ADC5722/2022, specifically including the First Respondent’s Application for a Harmful Proceedings Order pursuant to s102QAC, be stayed pending the determination of the Appellant’s Notice of Appeal.
That order was dismissed in circumstances where, as earlier identified, there is presently no appeal instituted by the father pending before the Court. The Court understands from submissions made by the father during the hearing that he had not, at the time of the hearing, rectified his failure to file a competent fee exemption and/or pay the filing fee, but that he intended to do so.
Having indicated to the father that the hearing would proceed in relation to all of the applications which had been listed before the Court, the Court attempted to ascertain from the father which, if any, of his extant applications before the Court he pressed. In the face of the father declining to press any of his extant applications nor make submissions in relation to each of them, the Court indicated to the father that it would deem those applications abandoned. The father declined the opportunity to make any submissions in relation to each of his extant interlocutory applications and accordingly the same were deemed abandoned and were dismissed. The father’s failure to make submissions, despite being given the opportunity to do so is telling and speaks to the possibility that those various applications may well have been instituted by the father as an abuse of process.
It was apparent during the hearing on 1 April 2026 that the father maintained his position that the hearing ought be adjourned. That application however had already been determined and dismissed by the Court pursuant to the orders made on 30 March 2026. Additionally, and despite the father asserting that he had medical issues that prevented his ability to appear and make submissions at the hearing (the same having grounded his earlier adjournment application), during the hearing it was apparent that the father was able to make submissions and meaningfully engage in the hearing; despite variously declining the opportunity to make submissions.
Whatever the case, the only remaining application before the Court following the dismissal of the father’s various interlocutory applications and the adjournment of the recently filed applications, was that contained in the mother’s Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 27 February 2026 in which she sought a harmful proceedings order directed to the father.

The power of the Court to make a harmful proceedings order is found in s 102QAC of the Act (which is found in Part XIB of the Act), which provides:

(b) in the case of child‑related proceedings (within the meaning of Part VII)—the child who is the subject of the proceedings would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party.

Note: Proceedings includes cross‑proceedings and incidental proceedings (see subsection 4(1)).
(2) For the purposes of subsection (1), harm may include, but is not limited to, the following:
(a) psychological harm or oppression;

(b) major mental distress;

(c) a detrimental effect on the other party’s capacity to care for a child;

(d) financial harm.
(3) In determining whether to make an order under subsection (1), the court may have regard to:
(a) the history of the proceedings under this Act between the first party and the other party; and

(b) whether the first party has frequently instituted or conducted proceedings against the other party in any Australian court or tribunal (including proceedings instituted (or attempted to be instituted) or conducted, and orders made, before the commencement of this section); and

(c) the cumulative effect, or any potential cumulative effect, of any harm resulting from the proceedings referred to in paragraphs (a) and (b).

(4) The court may make a harmful proceedings order on its own initiative or on application by a party to the proceedings.

(5) The court must not make a harmful proceedings order in relation to a person without hearing the person or giving the person an opportunity of being heard.

(6) An order made under subsection (1) is a final order.

Order about notifying other party in relation to application for leave etc.
(7) If the court makes an order under subsection (1), the court must also make an order as to whether the court is to notify the other party, in the event that the first party makes an application under section 102QAE for leave to institute proceedings against the other party, of either or both of the following:
(a) that the application has been made;
(b) if the application is dismissed—that the application has been dismissed.
(8) The court must have regard to the wishes of the other party in making an order under subsection (7).
The decision whether or not to make a harmful proceedings order is discretionary, so long as the preconditions in s 102QAC(1) and (5) of the Act are met.
The power to make a harmful proceedings order was a new provision inserted in the Act when amendments came into force on 6 May 2024, as a result of the Family Law Amendment Act 2023 (Cth).
The Explanatory Memorandum to the Family Law Amendment Bill 2023 (Cth), on the topic of harmful proceedings orders, relevantly variously identified:
(a) In the General Outline:
The Bill will provide a new mechanism for the court to respond to and deter a known form of harassment, that being the repetitive filing of applications by one party to oppress another party. Often termed ‘systems abuse’, this tactic can be employed by perpetrators of family violence to manipulate the legal system to exert control over, threaten and harass a current or former partner. This can have substantial detrimental impacts on the physical and psychological health of the respondent, and on any children involved in proceedings. The Bill will provide the courts with a new power to scrutinise the impact of the institution of further proceedings to prevent systems abuse occurring.

(b) And specifically in relation to the relevant provisions:

Item 6 also inserts a new Division 1B [sic] which contains provisions relating to the court’s new power to make harmful proceedings orders. The purpose of this measure is to protect the respondent and/or children who are the subject of proceedings from the harmful impact of frequent and unnecessary applications filed by an applicant. This measure aims to limit systems abuse, which is a form of family violence that is prevalent in the family law system.

DOCUMENTS BEFORE THE COURT

At the hearing the Court indicated to the parties, prior to hearing submissions with respect to the mother’s application, that the whole of the court file was before the Court, when considering whether to make a harmful proceedings order directed to the father (in accordance with s 102QAC(3)).
During the hearing, the mother identified that she relied upon her Response to an Application in a Proceeding and Affidavit filed 27 February 2026 together with written submissions filed on her behalf on 26 March 2026. Regard has been had to all of those documents, save and except that the father objected to the mother relying on annexure “[MSA]-7” to her affidavit of 27 February 2026. Regard has not been had to the same.
During the hearing the father sought to rely on written submissions he had filed on each 26 March 2026 and 30 March 2026. Regard has been had to those submissions.
The Court has otherwise had regard to the oral submissions made during the hearing. Significantly, the father was given the opportunity to make submissions, and he was heard in opposition to the making of the harmful proceedings order.

NOTICE THAT THE COURT WAS CONSIDERING MAKING A HARMFUL PROCEEDINGS ORDER

Whilst the father was on notice that the mother sought a harmful proceedings order as a consequence of the orders sought in her Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 27 February 2026 (to the father’s Application in a Proceeding of 22 December 2025 which was already listed for hearing on 1 April 2026), out of an abundance of caution, on 16 March 2026 orders were made in chambers so as to ensure firstly, that the father was aware that the issue would be heard at the hearing on 1 April 2026, but also directing the father to the relevant provisions in the Act given he is a self-represented litigant. The relevant orders of 16 March 2026 provided as follows:
In addition to the competing applications referred to in order 2 herein, the parties are directed to be in a position to make submissions at the hearing on 1 April 2026 as to whether a harmful proceedings order (pursuant to Part XIB, Division 1B of the Family Law Act (1975)) be made directed to the father.

DISCUSSION

The mother’s written submissions correctly identify (per Brasch J in Carey & Prescott (No 2) [2024] FedCFamC1F 512 at [164]):
... it has long been accepted that on-going litigation about a child is likely to inflict “enormous psychological harm ... upon each other [the adults] but especially upon the child” (McEnerney & McEnerney [1980] FamCA 43; (1980) FLC 90-866 per Nygh J at 75,499 (“McEnerney”) (emphasis added).
The mother submits that the father “appears to have no insight into the potential deleterious and harmful effects of relentless applications” on the mother (mother’s written submissions filed 26 March 2026, p.7).
By her affidavit filed 27 February 2026 (and summarised in her written submissions), the mother deposes:
(a) The father has taken video recordings of the child which he has attempted to play in Court and has otherwise sent to the mother, including the father coaxing the child to say that he wants to “stay overnight” with the father, causing the mother distress, and fearing for the impact of the father’s behaviour on the child (paragraphs 69 and 70).

(b) That in addition to the proceedings in this Court, the father has instituted proceedings in the Administrative Review Tribunal regarding the child support assessment which remain before the tribunal and have been running concurrently with these proceedings adding to the mother’s “anxiety” and “stress” (paragraph 66). The father is currently in arrears of child support in the amount of $989.14, with no payment having been made by the father for a period of four months increasing the financial pressure she is experiencing.

(c) She has been so overwhelmed by these proceedings that at times she has been unable to read the documents provided to her by her lawyer, making the point that she is slower in reading English (she is from Country C and came to Australia to pursue the relationship with the father after meeting online).

(d) That she had anticipated that following the making of orders by consent on 11 November 2025 that the tide of this litigation would abate, but that has not occurred and she has spent “many hours” taking advice from and giving instructions to her solicitors (paragraph 94).

(e) She has deferred her studies as the stress of this litigation was too much of a distraction for her.

(f) She is aware from the material filed by the father that he has made “numerous complaints about his lawyers, my lawyer, David Childs [the mother’s counsel], the Independent children’s lawyer, judges and even the Chief Justice in these proceedings”, including referrals to the Legal Conduct Commissioner (paragraph 90).

Annexed to the mother’s affidavit of 27 February 2026 (and marked “[MSA]-8”), the mother produced a report from her treating psychologist Ms H dated 12 February 2026. Whilst the father argued that the Court ought reject that document, given the interlocutory nature of these proceedings, and that these are parenting proceedings, it is appropriate for the Court to receive and have regard to the same.
By her report Ms H records:
(a) The “court proceedings and mediation proceedings” had “adversely affected” the mother;

(b) The increased number of applications in the proceedings had resulted in the mother “becoming extremely stressed”; and

(c) The mother’s current stress levels are “very high”.

Significantly, Ms H opined in the report that the “uncertainty” created by these proceedings “diminishes [the mother’s] emotional safety”.
Taking all of these matters into account, significant concern exists as to the deleterious impact upon the mother of the father’s conduct generally and associated with these proceedings. Specifically concern is held that the mother is already suffering significant emotional and/or psychological distress and harm as a result of the father’s conduct, and that such harm will only be exacerbated if the father was permitted to conduct the within litigation in the manner which he has been conducting it to date, and which appears to have heightened in intensity since December 2025.
The Court is therefore satisfied that it is appropriate to make a harmful proceedings order directed to the father.
As requested by the mother, orders are also made that the mother is not to be notified of any applications for leave to institute proceedings made by the father and any determination of the same.
